career.notes: Per Goodyear book #6852-1: 'Colonel Blakeslee was for many years an active and enthusiastic member of the Connecticut Militia.' Title 'Colonel' used throughout.

narrative_blocks.header: Polly died of grief weeks after daughter Harriet
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Harriet — 'an attractive and lovable girl' per the Goodyear book — died at age eighteen on 12 September 1876. His wife Polly Goodyear Blakeslee died less than two weeks later on 25 September 1876, attributed by the family record to grief.

narrative_blocks.header: Polly's last words: dying bed soft as pillows
narrative_blocks.body: The Goodyear book preserves Polly's last words: 'Jesus can make a dying bed feel soft as downy pillows are, while on his breast I'll lean my head, and breathe my life out sweetly there.' The lines come from an Isaac Watts hymn circulating widely in nineteenth-century New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife Cornelia was Polly's Bradley cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Polly's death he married Cornelia Bronson Andrews on 7 December 1878. Cornelia and Polly were Bradley cousins — both great-granddaughters of Titus and Lydia Todd Bradley — making the second wife a documented blood relation of the first.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived ninety-four years, Connecticut Militia Colonel
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Merwin Blakeslee was born in Plymouth Connecticut on August 17, 1814 and died in North Haven on February 11, 1909, age ninety-four. He served many years in the Connecticut Militia and was widely known by the title Colonel; the Goodyear book noted he still led 'an energetic life in North Haven' in his eighties.

career.notes: Per Goodyear book #6852-1: 'Colonel Blakeslee was for many years an active and enthusiastic member of the Connecticut Militia.' Title 'Colonel' used throughout.

The lines come from an Isaac Watts hymn circulating widely in nineteenth-century New England.

narrative_blocks.body: Henry Merwin Blakeslee was born in Plymouth Connecticut on August 17, 1814 and died in North Haven on February 11, 1909, age ninety-four. He served many years in the Connecticut Militia and was widely known by the title Colonel; the Goodyear book noted he still led 'an energetic life in North Haven' in his eighties.

bio.bio_blurb: New Haven mother who died of grief

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Andrew Goodyear (1784-1819) and Patty Bradley (1782-1821), both of Hamden CT (Goodyear book #6852). Andrew d. when Polly was age 4; Patty d. when Polly was age 6. After parents' deaths, Polly lived with her grandmother Bradley in North Haven. Mother Patty's line traces through Titus + Lydia (Todd) Bradley of N. Haven; same Bradley line as HMB's second wife Cornelia Bronson Andrews (I00534), so Polly and Cornelia are Bradley cousins. Goodyear line also produced significant Hooker connections elsewhere — Polly's brother William Bradley Goodyear m. Charlotte Frost (Hooker descendant via H00448 Mabel Stiles Frost). NOTE: This Goodyear line does NOT directly descend from Thomas Hooker; Polly is non-Hooker spouse to HMB.

narrative_blocks.header: She died of grief days after her daughter
narrative_blocks.body: When her eighteen-year-old daughter Harriet died in September 1876, she followed less than two weeks later; her family laid her death to grief. They lie together in the New Haven cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Orphaned at six, raised by her grandmother
narrative_blocks.body: Both her parents were dead before she turned six, and her Bradley grandmother in New Haven raised her. A family chronicle remembered her as firm and gentle at once, with an unshakable faith.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother founded Ogden, Utah
narrative_blocks.body: One of her brothers, Miles Goodyear, became a Western trapper who founded what is now Ogden, Utah, and married a daughter of a Ute chief — a far cry from the New Haven world she kept to all her life.

quotes.text: Jesus can make a dying bed feel soft as downy pillows are, while on His breast I'll lean my head, and breathe my life out sweetly there.
quotes.source: Reported as Polly Goodyear Blakeslee's last words in the Goodyear book #6852-1, drawn from Isaac Watts hymn 'Why should we start, and fear to die?'

narrative_blocks.header: Married at forty-five, fathered twin sons at fifty-six
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore M. Blakeslee did not marry until February 9, 1885, age forty-five, when he wed Susan Anne 'Susie' Drake (1859-1933) at Middleborough Massachusetts. Their first daughter Hattie May was born ten months later in November 1885 but died age five in 1891. After their daughter Lottie was born in 1889, Theodore and Susan had twin sons Merwin Theodore Blakeslee and Miles Edwin Blakeslee on the same day, July 23, 1896 — Theodore was fifty-six.

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War veteran; body returned to North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Theodore Blakeslee served as a Civil War veteran — specific unit and rank are not documented in surviving records, but his veteran status is confirmed. He died January 5, 1901 in New York City at age sixty-one. His body was returned to his Connecticut hometown of North Haven for burial at New Center Cemetery, where the Blakeslee-Stiles family clustered four generations of graves around the Stiles-Hooker line center.

narrative_blocks.header: Hattie died young
narrative_blocks.body: Hattie died in 1891 at the age of five.

narrative_blocks.header: Only daughter Stella died at age thirty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Lottie's only documented daughter Stella Tuttle (born August 17, 1863) died November 1, 1894 at age thirty-one — predeceasing her mother by seven and a half years. Stella had married Fred A. Warner on January 27, 1887 and they had one son, Leonard B. Warner (born May 14, 1889). The single-generation maternal-line continuation through Leonard was all that survived from Lottie's marriage to Julian Tuttle.

narrative_blocks.header: Lottie was the nickname used in family records
narrative_blocks.body: Charlotte E. Blakeslee was known throughout family records as 'Lottie' — the standard nineteenth-century diminutive of Charlotte. She married Julian W. Tuttle of North Haven Connecticut, who was born April 16, 1836 and died April 10, 1907 — slightly outliving Lottie.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Connecticut 1844, died North Haven 1902
narrative_blocks.body: Charlotte E. Blakeslee was born April 17, 1844 in Connecticut and died March 19, 1902 in North Haven Connecticut at age fifty-seven. She is buried at New Center Cemetery. Daughter of Colonel Henry Merwin Blakeslee and Polly Goodyear Blakeslee.

narrative_blocks.header: Died young leaving five-year-old son Leonard Warner, 1894
narrative_blocks.body: Stella Tuttle Warner died on November 1, 1894 at age 31, seven years after her January 1887 marriage to Fred Warner. She left a single five-year-old son, Leonard Warner, who was the great-grandson of Col. Henry Merwin Blakeslee.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Joel E. Todd and Amanda (Clark) Todd per Goodyear book #6852-1. Both non-Hooker, not built. Surname Todd may share family ties with the Todd line that includes Polly Goodyear's maternal grandmother Lydia (Todd) Bradley — connection not documented in available source.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age eighteen, mother followed thirteen days later
narrative_blocks.body: Harriet Blakeslee was born November 23, 1857 and died September 12, 1876 age eighteen, described by the Goodyear family record as 'an attractive and lovable girl.' Her mother Polly Goodyear Blakeslee died thirteen days afterward on September 25, 1876, the family attributing her death to grief.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Jesse Andrews and Mary Jenette (Bradley) Andrews. Per Goodyear book #6852-1: granddaughter of Titus Jr. + Mary Munson Bradley; great-granddaughter of Titus + Lydia (Todd) Bradley — same Titus + Lydia Bradley great-grandparents as HMB's first wife Polly Goodyear's mother Patty Bradley. So Cornelia and Polly were Bradley cousins (Cornelia's great-grandmother = Polly's grandmother, making them first cousins twice removed via the Bradley line). Non-Hooker.

Son of Amos Blakeslee (1759-1827) and Eunice (Cooper) Blakeslee per Goodyear book. Both non-Hooker, not built. NOTE: Eunice Cooper — possibly related to Lois Cooper (I00524, HD3922 Isaac Stiles's wife, dau of Justus + Lois Cooper of N. Haven). Both Cooper families in N. Haven same era; relationship not documented in available source.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Henry Merwin Blakeslee and Sophronia Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Frost Blakeslee was daughter of Mabel Stiles Frost and Titus Frost of North Haven; she married Anson Blakeslee in 1810 and bore six children. Two of those children's marriages knit the Hooker descendants of the Isaac Stiles sister-branches back together: Sophronia wed Isaac Lorenzo Stiles, and Lavinia's son Anson later married Mary Cyrilla Stiles.

parents.father_research_notes: Ebenezer Frost (1707–~1757) of North Haven CT — non-Hooker, not built.
parents.mother_research_notes: Damaris Ives Frost (1718–1802) of North Haven CT — non-Hooker, not built.

career.notes: Per Frost Family genealogy (BIB007): had 'full charge of the music in St. John's Church at No. Haven, Conn.' from 1783; called 'the father of music' in 1775.
Per Frost Family genealogy: chairmaker at Muddy River; church membership records from 1763 note he made coffins for ten shillings each.

narrative_blocks.header: 'Father of music' at North Haven, 1775
narrative_blocks.body: Church records as early as 1763 note he made coffins for ten shillings each. From 1783 he directed the music at St. John's Church North Haven while working as a chairmaker at Muddy River.

sources.page: Memorial at Montowese Cemetery, North Haven CT

narrative_blocks.header: Husband Titus Frost called father of music
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel's husband Titus Frost was known locally as 'the father of music at North Haven' — the recognized musical leader of the small Connecticut town that had emerged from the colonial Quinnipiac settlement. He also worked as a chairmaker at Muddy River in New Haven, combining the musical reputation with the craftsman trade of an established North Haven householder. The dual identity of musician and chairmaker placed him among the multi-trade artisans typical of post-Revolutionary Connecticut village economies.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven children documented from 1773 marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel and Titus Frost married July 29, 1773 in North Haven Connecticut and had seven documented children — Samuel, Willard 'William,' Polly, Julia, John, Leverett, and Titus Jr. The substantial completed family size was typical of the post-Revolutionary North Haven Stiles-Frost generation, with most of the children remaining in or near the town through their adult lives.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Haven 1753, died there 1834
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Stiles Frost was born February 12, 1753 in North Haven Connecticut — the village then still called Quinnipiac — and died there May 1, 1834 at age eighty-one. Her father Isaac Stiles Jr. and her grandmother Esther Hooker connected her to the Stiles-Hooker line that ran continuously through the eighteenth-century North Haven community.

burialCemetery.name: New Center Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cemetery in North Haven CT. Major Stiles family burial ground spanning multiple generations — Isaac Stiles (HD3922) + Lois Cooper (I00524); infant son Sterling (HD3933); son Isaac Lorenzo (HD3934). Additional Stiles burials to be added in subsequent stages.
